content:
# Revelation Chapter 1 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus, page 278 | **Source Image:** REV_001_page_001.jpg

**1** This is the unveiling of Jesus Christ -- a revelation God gave him so he could show his servants what must happen soon. He made it known by sending his angel to his servant John,

**2** who now testifies to everything he saw: the word of God and the testimony of Jesus Christ.

**3** Blessed is the one who reads these prophetic words aloud, and blessed are those who hear them and take to heart what is written here, for the time of fulfillment is near.

**4** John, to the seven churches in the province of Asia: Grace and peace to you from the One who is, who was, and who is coming, and from the seven spirits before his throne,

**5** and from Jesus Christ, the faithful witness, the firstborn from among the dead, and the ruler over the kings of the earth. To the one who loves us and has freed us from our sins by his own blood --

**6** who made us a kingdom, priests serving his God and Father -- to him be glory and dominion forever and ever. Amen.

**7** Look! He is coming with the clouds, and every eye will see him, even those who pierced him, and all the peoples of the earth will beat their chests in grief because of him. Yes indeed. Amen.

**8** "I am the Alpha and the Omega," says the Lord God, "the One who is, who was, and who is coming -- the Almighty."

**9** I, John, your brother and partner in the suffering, the kingdom, and the patient endurance that are ours in Jesus -- I was on the island called Patmos because of the word of God and the testimony of Jesus.

**10** I was caught up in the Spirit on the Lord's Day, and I heard behind me a tremendous voice, like a trumpet blast,

**11** saying, "Write down what you see in a scroll and send it to the seven churches: to Ephesus, Smyrna, Pergamum, Thyatira, Sardis, Philadelphia, and Laodicea."

**12** I turned to see who was speaking to me, and when I turned I saw seven golden lampstands,

**13** and standing among the lampstands was someone like a son of man, dressed in a robe reaching to his feet, with a golden sash wrapped around his chest.

**14** His head and his hair were white as wool, white as snow, and his eyes blazed like fire.

**15** His feet gleamed like bronze refined in a furnace, and his voice thundered like the roar of many waters.

**16** In his right hand he held seven stars, and from his mouth came a sharp, double-edged sword. His face shone like the sun blazing at full strength.

**17** When I saw him, I collapsed at his feet as though dead. But he placed his right hand on me and said, "Do not be afraid. I am the First and the Last,

**18** the Living One. I was dead, and look -- I am alive forever and ever! And I hold the keys of Death and of the grave.

**19** So write down what you have seen, both what is happening now and what will happen after this.

**20** As for the mystery of the seven stars you saw in my right hand and the seven golden lampstands: the seven stars are the angels of the seven churches, and the seven lampstands are the seven churches themselves."

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

This opening chapter of the Apocalypse establishes the cosmic scope of the revelation. Several dynamic choices deserve explanation:

- **"Unveiling"** (v. 1): The Greek ΑΠΟΚΑΛΥΨΙΣ literally means "uncovering" or "removal of a veil." I chose "unveiling" to capture the sense that something hidden is being dramatically disclosed, rather than the Latinized "revelation" which has lost its force through familiarity.

- **"Beat their chests in grief"** (v. 7): The Greek ΚΟΨΟΝΤΑΙ (from kopto) means to cut or strike oneself in mourning. Rather than the pale "mourn," I rendered this with the physical gesture of grief that the original conveys -- chest-beating lamentation, a response of visceral anguish.

- **"Caught up in the Spirit"** (v. 10): ΕΓΕΝΟΜΗΝ ΕΝ ΠΝΕΥΜΑΤΙ suggests not mere contemplation but an ecstatic transport. "Caught up" conveys the involuntary, overwhelming nature of this spiritual experience.

- **"Someone like a son of man"** (v. 13): I retained the Danielic allusion (Dan 7:13) rather than paraphrasing it away. The phrase is deliberately mysterious in both Daniel and Revelation -- it identifies the figure as both human and more-than-human.

- **"The grave"** (v. 18): The Greek ΑΔΟΥ (Hades) is not "hell" in the later theological sense but the realm of the dead. "The grave" captures the domain over which Christ now claims authority without importing later doctrinal freight.

- Sinaiticus notably reads "freed us" (ΛΥΣΑΝΤΙ) in v. 5 rather than the later variant "washed us" (ΛΟΥΣΑΝΤΙ), a single-letter difference in Greek with significant theological implications. I follow the Sinaiticus reading.

-- PNEUMA
